sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 56-year-old male, presents with a chief complaint of difficulty swallowing, which he describes as a sensation of something being stuck in his throat for the past 4-5 weeks.
He reports that it is more noticeable when eating solids, such as meat or sandwiches, and can be painful.
He has not experienced any coughing or vomiting blood.
He has a past medical history of diabetes and high blood pressure, for which he is currently taking metformin 500mg twice daily and norvasc.
His hemoglobin A1c was previously measured at 7.4.
He reports that his blood sugars have been within target range, but he occasionally skips checking his blood glucose levels.
He denies any nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure is 120/50 mmHg.
Physical exam: The patient's neck is fine, with no swelling or masses.
The thyroid gland feels normal, and there are no lymph nodes.
The throat examination is normal, with no masses, swelling, or redness.
The lung and heart exams are clear, with no murmurs.
The abdomen exam reveals epigastric tenderness in the right upper quadrant, but no masses or significant swelling.
The back and extremity exams are normal, and the neurological exam is fine.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has difficulty swallowing (esophageal dysphagia) likely due to a narrowing of the esophagus, possibly a stricture.
Diabetes (uncontrolled) with hemoglobin A1c of 7.4; high blood pressure (controlled) on norvasc.
Plan:
I will refer the patient to gastroenterology for further evaluation and possible endoscopy to investigate the cause of his difficulty swallowing.
I will also prescribe prilosec 20mg once daily for reflux.
I recommend that the patient chew his food frequently, especially when eating solids, to prevent getting stuck.
I will order another hemoglobin A1c, kidney function tests, and refer the patient to ophthalmology for diabetic retinopathy screening.

I will also continue the patient's current medication regimen, including metformin and norvasc, and schedule a follow-up appointment in one month to review the results of the blood tests and make any necessary adjustments.
Follow-up:
The patient will return in one month for a follow-up appointment to review the results of the blood tests and make any necessary adjustments to his medication regimen.
